ontology.foodex2_description: Beef, loin, tenderloin, separable lean and fat, cooked, broiled

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of grilled beef fillet is approximately 300g, comprising roughly 150–180g of beef tenderloin plus brown sauce, salad, and a side of butter rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Beef Fillet — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Beef Fillet (G00010178), a grilled marinated beef tenderloin dish served with brown sauce, salad, and choice of butter rice or bread. All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate for mixed beef dish with minor carbohydrate components
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: Beef fillet itself has negligible intrinsic carbohydrate and no direct GI entry. The dish contains 4.19g carbohydrate per 100g from marinade, brown sauce, and accompaniment components. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on the analogous mixed meat-and-sauce dish category from the University of Sydney GI Database, reflecting the low-GI contribution of the sauce and marinade carbohydrates. Glycemic load per 300g serving = (45 × 4.19 × 3) / 100 ≈ 5.7; per 100g portion GL = (45 × 4.19) / 100 ≈ 1.9, rounded to 2.5 for the realistic 130g beef-only sub-portion. Documented as analogous estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 369 mg/100g with partial potassium offset (368 mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 369 mg/100g sits in the borderline range for hypertension; patients should request preparation without added salt or high-sodium sauces (e.g., soy sauce, steak sauce), and the meaningful potassium content (368 mg/100g) provides partial vascular offset, but total daily sodium budget must still be monitored.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High protein (18.48 g/100g), low carbohydrate (4.19 g/100g), low sugar (1.45 g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The high protein and low carbohydrate profile of beef fillet supports glycemic stability in Type 2 Diabetes; patients should limit portion to 100–120 g of beef and avoid pairing with high-GI sides such as white rice or mashed potato to maintain postprandial glucose control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 2.3 g/100g; cholesterol 52 mg/100g; red meat classification
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Beef tenderloin is a lean red meat with moderate saturated fat (~2.3 g/100g) and cholesterol (52 mg/100g); cardiovascular disease patients may consume lean beef fillet in portions of 100 g or less 1–2 times per week, prepared without butter or cream-based sauces, and should prioritise olive oil-based preparation to minimise LDL-raising saturated fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat arachidonic acid (omega-6) amplifies TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Beef is a red meat rich in arachidonic acid, which promotes pro-inflammatory eicosanoid production relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; patients with active RA should limit or avoid beef fillet and consider substituting with oily fish (salmon, mackerel) for anti-inflammatory omega-3 benefits.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Fillet is a grilled marinated beef tenderloin dish served with brown sauce, salad, and a choice of butter rice or bread. The tenderloin is the most tender cut of beef, taken from the psoas major muscle along the spine, which bears minimal weight and therefore develops very little connective tissue. The dish is typically marinated in a blend of herbs, spices, and oil before being grilled at high heat to develop a caramelised exterior while retaining a juicy, tender interior. The brown sauce — typically a demi-glace or jus-based reduction — adds umami depth and a slight sweetness. Per 100g, the dish provides 148 kcal, 18.48g protein, 6.0g fat (approximately 2.3g saturated), 4.19g carbohydrate, 1.45g sugar, 0.8g dietary fibre, and 369mg sodium. The high protein content supports satiety and muscle maintenance. The low carbohydrate and sugar profile makes it suitable for glycaemic management in Type 2 Diabetes when paired with low-GI sides. Sodium at 369mg/100g is borderline for hypertension and warrants sauce moderation. As a red meat, it carries a moderate cardiovascular concern due to saturated fat and cholesterol (52mg/100g), and a high concern for rheumatoid arthritis due to arachidonic acid content. The dish is commonly served at lunch and dinner in Singaporean Western-style restaurants and hotel dining venues, and is associated with Middle Eastern-influenced marinades in the HPB Singapore food database context.
common_user_queries: How many calories in beef fillet?
Is beef tenderloin high in sodium?
Is beef fillet good for weight loss?
Can I eat beef fillet with high blood pressure?
Is beef fillet suitable for heart disease?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry G00010178 with confidence 0.95. The record is marked as reviewed and provenance is canonical. Saturated fat estimated at 2.3g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central beef tenderloin data (NDB 23189) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown separately; this single field is adapted rather than exact.
notes: Saturated fat (2.3g/100g) is an adapted estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central beef tenderloin entry (NDB 23189) as the HPB source does not report saturated fat separately. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish and detailed fatty acid, vitamin, and moisture breakdowns for this specific prepared dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026. The input record listed primary_cuisine as 'Middle Eastern'; this has been reclassified to 'International / Western' as beef fillet / tenderloin is a globally recognised Western preparation; the Middle Eastern tag is retained in the cultural context of the HPB record and may reflect marinade style. GI is an analogous estimate (low, ~45) as no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish; the estimate is based on the low carbohydrate content (4.19g/100g) and the University of Sydney GI Database analogous mixed meat-and-sauce category. Halal flag set to true as beef tenderloin is halal when sourced and prepared according to Islamic dietary law; this should be verified at point of service. No zero-value core macros are present in this record.
